# What is IoT Data Collection?

## Definition

IoT data collection is the process of using sensors to track the conditions of physical things. Devices and technology connected over the Â Internet of Things (IoT) Â can monitor and measure data in real time. The data are transmitted, stored, and can be retrieved at any time.

## Types of IoT Data

There are three common types of IoT data in the commercial real estate industry.

This type of data enables real-time fault detection, runtime-based schedules and predictive maintenance thus saving energy cost, increasing productivity and extending equipment life. Equipment data can be centralized in one platform so that on-site staff, management, and vendors can all be on the same page and data can rollup into executive dashboards.

IoT sensors can be deployed to track a range of data streams within buildings: temperature, air quality, people flow, moisture, or movement. These datasets are primarily used to be proactive for occupant comfort issues and avoid disaster scenarios such as leaks and floods.

Digital Submetering devices connected to the internet help automate the utility submetering process which Â costs, eliminates errors and generates bills as soon as the billing period ends. Submetering provides transparency to portfolio owners for the recovery rates of their cash outlays.

## Why it Matters

There are already more connected things than people in the world.

Experts predict that in total there will be 41.6 billion connected IoT devices by 2025. Industrial, automotive, and commercial building equipment represent the largest segment, but there is also strong adoption of smart home and wearable devices. Â

The Internet of Things has allowed companies to significantly widen the range of data that they are able to capture.

One of the most prominent examples of this is in the real estate industry. IoT data helps homes, offices, retail stores, and institutions to optimize building performance by

![Enertiv IoT data collection, IoT, internet of things](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/662247a741408fe58d03da3e/664ededd0116129fe63e0b37_Faults%252BWe%252BDetect%252B%25284%2529%2B%25281%2529.webp)

These give portfolio managers transparency where it never existed before and often provides insights that lead to significant cost savings.

# How IoT Data Collection Works [Complete Guide 2024]

![Iot data collection](https://www.dusuniot.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/Iot-data-collection.jpg)

The revolution of the Internet of Things (IoT) has dramatically transformed how data is collected, processed, and used.  Through interconnected devices, IoT allows for the collection, transmission, and analysis of huge amounts of data, leading to smarter decisions and more efficient operations across various industries.

This data collection forms the foundation of IoT, driving advancements in industries ranging from healthcare to manufacturing. This article delves into the concept of IoT data collection, its types, benefits, challenges, principles, architecture, use cases, and future trends.

## What Is IoT data collection?

IoT data collection is the process of gathering data from a network of interconnected devices and sensors that communicate with each other over the internet. These devices, often embedded in objects, monitor and measure real-time data on various parameters, such as movement, humidity, temperature, usage patterns, etc.

The collected IoT data is transmitted to central systems where it can be processed and analyzed to extract actionable insights. This data can be stored and retrieved at any moment in time.

## Types of IoT data collection

### Automation data

Automation IoT data collection entails gathering information from processes and systems that operate autonomously (without human intervention. This type of data is important in industrial settings where automated machinery and production lines are monitored to ensure optimal performance. By collecting IoT data on machine status, operational parameters, and performance metrics, organizations can identify inefficiencies, predict maintenance needs, optimize, and prevent downtime.

### Equipment data

Equipment IoT data collection entails the collections of data from various types of IoT devices, machinery, and equipment. This data includes metrics such as usage patterns, operational status, and performance benchmarks. The equipment IoT data is collected in real time to facilitate [predictive maintenance](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/what-is-iot-predictive-maintenance/), ensuring that machines operate efficiently and reducing downtime.

### Environmental data

Environmental data collection entails gathering information IoT data about physical conditions, such as temperature, humidity, air quality, and light levels. This type of IoT data collection is vital in sectors like [smart agriculture](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/build-smart-agriculture-fields-and-farms-with-lorawan-gateways/), where soil moisture and weather conditions need to be monitored, and in [smart buildings](https://www.dusuniot.com/solution/smart-apartment-solution/) to maintain optimal living conditions.

### Location data

Location data collection entails gathering of IoT data from devices and sensors equipped with GPS or other positional technologies. This IoT data provides real-time insights into the [geographical location](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/ble-beacon-location-tracking-for-asset-management/), movement of individuals or assets, employee monitoring, and other management tasks. Location IoT data collection is widely used in logistics, [asset management](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/ble-beacon-location-tracking-for-asset-management/) and [asset tracking](https://www.dusuniot.com/product/iot-based-inventory-management-and-asset-tracking-system/), supply chain management, and navigating systems.

## What are the benefits of IoT data collection?

Proper IoT data collection can deliver numerous benefits to organizations:

## Challenges of IoT data collection

Despite its benefits, IoT data collection presents several challenges, including the following:

### Data security and privacy concerns

Ensuring data security and privacy is a major concern in IoT data collection because of the sensitive nature of the data generated by [IoT devices](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/remote-management-of-iot-devices-a-boon-for-device-manufacturers/) and [smart sensors](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/smart-sensor/). Therefore, you must implement robust data governance practices, access controls, and encryption protocols to protect IoT from unauthorized access, cyber threats, and data breaches.

Moreover, compliance with data protection regulations like CCPA and GDPR is critical in safeguarding user privacy and maintaining trust in IoT data collection systems.

### Scalability challenges with large data volumes

Managing the scalability issues related with huge volumes of IoT data is another critical challenge in IoT data collection. As the number of data sources and connected IoT devices grows, organizations should scale their data analytics, data processing, and data storage infrastructure to handle the increasing data loads.

Implementing [edge computing technologies](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/edge-computing-gateway), distributed computing framework, and scalable storage solutions can assist address scalability challenges and ensure optimal performance in IoT data collection systems.

## How IoT data collection works

The working of IoT data collection entails the following stages:

### Stage 1: Data generation

The first stage in IoT data collection entails collecting data from machines, devices, and sensors installed in various environments. This data include [location information](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/ble-beacon-location-tracking-for-asset-management/), device status updates, sensor readings, and other relevant metrics.

### Stage 2: Data transmission

Once IoT data is gathered, it needs to be transmitted to a central system or cloud server for further processing and analysis. This transmission is done using special translators, known as [IoT gateways](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/smart-gateways/). For instance, the gateways receives all the information and messages from smart sensors, performs preliminary data filtering and processing, and then transmits the data through wired or wireless networks (([LoRaWAN](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/lorawan-gateway/), [Zigbee](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/zigbee-gateways/), [Bluetooth](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/bluetooth-gateway/), [cellular networks](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/cellular-lte-gateways/)).

The IoT gateway also ensures data security through encryption and provides efficient transmission protocol. This protects data confidentiality and integrity during transmission.

Organizations can use on-premise servers, [edge computing solutions](https://www.dusuniot.com/landing-pages/edge-computing-gateway), and cloud platforms to receive and store the collected IoT data for subsequent processing and analysis.

For example, [ThingsBoard](https://thingsboard.io/) is an open-source IoT platform for data collection, processing, visualization, and device management.

### Stage 3: Data processing

The received data is processed and analyzed to extract meaningful insights. The processing includes data cleaning, organizing data insights, adding metadata to IoT data, etc.

### Stage 4: Data storage

The processed data is then stored in data lakes, and databases for analysis and visualization. Your chosen data storage solution must be capable of handling huge volumes of IoT data, secure, and scalable.

### Stage 5: Analysis and visualization

Analysis techniques like predictive analytics, machine learning, and data mining can be leveraged to derive actionable insights from the collected and stored IoT data. Moreover, visualization dashboards and tools allow organizations to interpret and communicate the findings of IoT data analysis.

## Methods of IoT data collection

### Telemetry data collection

Telemetry IoT data collection entails the continuous [remote monitoring](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/iot-remote-monitoring-managing-assets-and-tracking-conditions-from-anywhere-in-the-world/) and transmission of data from IoT sensors/devices in real time. This method captures data  about environmental conditions, operational status, and device performance.

The telemetry data is essential in enabling [predictive maintenance](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/what-is-iot-predictive-maintenance/), detecting anomalies, and monitoring device health in industrial [IoT solutions](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/iot-solution-empowering-the-future-with-revolutionary-impact/).

### Sensor data collection

**Sensor** data collection involves gathering information from different sensors embedded in IoT devices. These sensors collect physical parameters, like motion sensor, pressure, humidity, temperature, etc.

The sensor data offers valuable insights into the equipment performance, user behavior, and surrounding environment.

By aggregating and analyzing sensor data, organizations can enhance user experience, improve efficiency, and optimize processes.

### Historical data storage

The historical data storage entails retaining and archiving IoT data for regulatory compliance, trend analysis, and future analysis. This method of IoT data collection allows organizations to identify patterns, track performance over time, and make data-driven decisions according to historical trends.

We recommend using robust sta storage solutions to efficiently manage and securely store huge loads of historical IoT data.

## IoT Data collection architecture and technology

### Device Layer

This is the primary layer of the IoT ecosystem, consisting of devices that communicate with each other and collect data from the physical environment. These devices range from simple sensors monitoring physical conditions to complex machines monitoring industrial processes.

### Communication Layer

The Communication Layer ensures data transfer between the Device Layer and subsequent layers. Within this layer, IoT Gateways play a critical role. These gateways act as intermediaries, aggregating data from multiple devices and ensuring efficient communication with the IT Edge Layer.

The IoT gateways in this layer handle various communication technologies and protocols (HTTP/HTTPS, MQTT, and CoAP, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, Zigbee, and cellular networks). They also provide necessary security measures, such as data encryption and authentication, to ensure data integrity, efficiency, and reliability during transmission.

### IT Edge Layer

The IT Edge Layer processes data closer to the data source, reducing latency and bandwidth use.  It is made up of the firmware, [hardware systems](https://www.dusuniot.com/shop/), and operating system of the IoT devices.

IoT Gateways are again crucial in this layer. They perform preliminary data filtering and aggregation. Also, it sometimes performs edge analytics before forwarding the data to the central processing systems.

This layer’s proximity to data sources allows for real-time data processing, which is essential for applications requiring immediate response, such as industrial automation..

### Event Processing Layer

The event processing layer is responsible for processing and storing data from IoT devices to detect patterns, anomalies, and significant events. The processing that happens in this layer include data cleaning, organizing data insights, and adding metadata to IoT data.

This layer uses advanced analytics and machine learning algorithms to derive and to provide immediate insights and trigger automated responses.

### Client Communication Layer

The client communication layer manages interactions between the IoT system and end-users or applications. This includes dashboards, mobile apps, and APIs that allow users to access data, monitor, and analyze IoT data. It ensures that the right information is delivered to the right users or stakeholders.

## IoT data collection: Use cases

The following industries are among the frontrunners in making the most of IoT data collection:

## Future trends in IoT data collection

### Advancements in real-time data visualization

The advancements in real-time data visualization technologies will play a key role in advancing IoT data collection. For example, innovations in interactive dashboards, augmented reality, and data visualization tools will allow organizations to gain deeper insights from the collected IoT data in real time.

Moreover, enhanced visualization capabilities will allow for improved situation awareness in diverse IoT applications, proactive monitoring, and faster decision-making.

### Integration of machine learning and AI for data analysis

The future trends in IoT data collection will focus on the integration of machine learning and artificial intelligence in advancing IoT data collection and analysis. This will drive innovation in the data collection process and empower organizations to extract actionable insights from the collected IoT data.

AI-powered analytics tools will predict future trends, detect patterns, and automate data processing based on IoT data. Similarly, machine learning models will enable [predictive maintenance](https://www.dusuniot.com/blog/what-is-iot-predictive-maintenance/) in IoT systems, enhance anomaly detection, and optimize IoT data collection strategies.

# How Internet of Things Data Collection Works: A Full Guide

![How Internet of Things Data Collection Works: A Full Guide](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![How Internet of Things Data Collection Works: A Full Guide](https://webbylab.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/4-1-791x593.png)

![How Internet of Things Data Collection Works: A Full Guide](https://webbylab.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/4-1-791x593.png)

There are [over 15 billion IoT-connected devices worldwide](https://www.statista.com/statistics/1183457/iot-connected-devices-worldwide/) right now. Can you imagine how much data these gadgets generate? For an organization that plans to adopt the IoT or has already done so, proper Internet of Things data collection is no small feat.

Luckily, this article will guide you in data collection and analysis in IoT. We will explain a robust IoT data-gathering system’s core principles, layers, and architecture and IoT data collection methods. Also, we will outline the potential challenges you may encounter along the way.

 As an [experienced IoT vendor](https://webbylab.com/iot/), WebbyLab will share its profound expertise in IoT-based data collection using our [Smart Heat project](https://webbylab.com/cases/smartheat/) as an example. Read along to get valuable insights on this topic backed up by our in-depth knowledge.

Industrial heat pump enhancement

[Read more](/cases/smartheat/)

## Importance of the Right IoT Data Collection for Businesses

Proper data collection from Internet of Things devices can deliver numerous benefits to businesses. Here are the main ones:

On top of that, McKinsey [states](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/iot-value-set-to-accelerate-through-2030-where-and-how-to-capture-it) that IoT and data collection have significant economic-value potential for various industries. The company estimates that the Internet of Things can unlock from $5.5 trillion to $12.6 trillion by 2030, with B2B applications creating the majority (65%) of that value.

## Various Types of IoT Data You Can Collect

As you’ve seen the undeniable benefits of Internet of Things data collection systems, you might now wonder what IoT collects and how this data can be utilized. Let’s look at the core types of data:

## How Does IoT Data Collection Work?

The Internet of Things data collection system leverages several principles for its proper operation. They include the following ones:

### IoT Data Collection Stages: WebbyLab Experience

Our team at WebbyLab divides IoT data collection into two stages:

At this stage, we implement the firmware, which will receive the necessary data from the peripherals, i.e., sensors, controlled devices, built-in modules, etc., as part of our custom [IoT software development services](https://webbylab.com/iot/).

In this case, we may collect and store IoT data in two ways:

Both data storage options are viable for different cases:

Our experts provide smooth Internet of Things data collection and server-side storage at this stage.

First, we ensure the server supports the API for receiving data from all connected devices. Once received, this data must be stored and available for further processing. It is also worth considering that the data amount will typically be large, and the platform’s server part must be ready to withstand this load.

### IoT Data Collection Methods as Evidenced by WebbyLab’s Background

WebbyLab utilizes three methods for efficient IoT data collection. Here’s a description of each:

In such a scenario, we connect all IoT devices to a [local or remote MQTT broker](/blog/mqtt-brokers-choosing-the-right-one-for-your-iot-solution/), ensuring real-time [IOT device connectivity](https://webbylab.com/blog/top-iot-connectivity-technologies/).

This IoT data collection method simplifies firmware implementation for devices. They only need to connect to the broker and update their sensor and telemetry values without storing them in memory. The server, in turn, is responsible for collecting and saving the data.

WebbyLab already has a service that tracks broker updates and saves historical data to the InfluxDB database. It enables real-time collection and storage of device information without putting a heavy load on the gadget.

InfluxDB is specially designed for such tasks, and it’s scalable and provides access to data for the next level of the application. It also has an API that enables the filtering and aggregation of data. For example, we can study the average temperature for each day over the last six months.

There are many combinations of filters and aggregation functions in InfluxDB. The only drawback and complication of such a process is the guarantee of data delivery and preservation. In this approach, there are several stages at which data can be lost, so you should work out both the firmware itself and the server part carefully. Additionally, the IoT device must always be online.

In this approach, IoT data is stored in firmware memory. Our experts set the current time for the firmware to record data changes accurately. How does it work?

 The IoT device works in stand-alone mode and collects a certain amount of data during operation but may not have a connection to the server due to implementation logic or other reasons. Yet, since we set the current time, the firmware stores all events in memory with accurate timestamps. Once connected, the device transfers all data to the server, which records it in the database. Since each event is tied to a timestamp, the data will be available to the user or administrator with reference to the moment it occurred.

For this approach to work correctly, you need to work out the rotation of the stored data on the device. It prevents the accumulation of data and ensures that the controller’s memory isn’t completely consumed.

With this approach of IoT data collection, the device sends data to the server in real time if it is online, such as via MQTT. If it goes offline or loses connection, it stores the data on the built-in memory and sends it to the server when the connection is restored.

This approach should be used where there is a clear need for it, as it will take a long time to implement and often require support for different APIs.

Each of the three approaches is best suited for specific situations:

### IoT Data Collection Architecture

The Internet of Things data collection architecture consists of several layers. Here’s a brief description of each:

This layer comprises the IoT devices like sensors, cameras, or smart home appliances. The devices collect data from the physical environment and transmit it to the next layer. You can [connect IoT remotely](https://webbylab.com/blog/bluetooth-vs-wifi-for-iot-project/) through robust communication protocols, enabling seamless data transfer and control over devices.

This layer transfers data from the IoT devices to the processing systems. It includes communication protocols and networks like Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, MQTT, [Zigbee](https://webbylab.com/blog/what-is-zigbee-protocol-in-wireless-iot-networks/), etc.

This layer is located at the edge of the network, closer to the devices, and comprises the hardware, firmware, and operating systems of the IoT devices. It plays a crucial role in data processing for IoT, performing preliminary processing and analysis of the data collected from connected devices.

This layer processes and analyzes the data in real time, identifying patterns and anomalies and triggering appropriate actions or alerts. It also cleans the data, organizes datasets, and generates insights.

This layer communicates the analysis results to the end-users through dashboards, reports, or mobile applications. It’s essentially a bridge between the database and the user interface.

ll layers of the  IoT sensor data collection system are critical to create a complete end-to-end solution that gathers, processes, and delivers insights from IoT devices.

## Challenges of IoT Data Collection

As we said at the beginning of our post, handling proper IoT data acquisition and collection might be challenging. But if you learn about the potential obstacles beforehand, you can overcome them in your project effortlessly.

## Examples of IoT Data Collection Systems [Based on WebbyLab Experience]

Our project on the [heat pump enhancement Smart Heat](https://webbylab.com/cases/smartheat/) is an IoT data collection example that resulted in better preventive maintenance. With the help of our team, a heat pump manufacturer could collect telemetry remotely and access historical data from all connected sensors.

![Viewing the heat pump status through a dashboard.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%202835%201293'%3E%3C/svg%3E)![Viewing the heat pump status through a dashboard.](https://webbylab.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/1.png)  
Viewing the heat pump status through a dashboard.

![Viewing the heat pump status through a dashboard.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%202835%201293'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Viewing the heat pump status through a dashboard.](https://webbylab.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/1.png)

The manufacturer also reduced maintenance costs by connecting to a specific heat pump and receiving data from its sensors in real time. With our IoT solution, any occurring issues can be resolved remotely by making the necessary changes to the configuration of the equipment.

IoT data collection and storage is generally the primary focus of the WebbyLab team. As part of the Smart Heat project, our experts made it possible to store historical data from each sensor in the cloud by connecting the IoT devices to [our 2Smart Cloud platform](https://webbylab.com/cases/2smart/).

The manufacturer, in turn, can provide access to this information to their devices’ end users. All that’s necessary is to configure the mobile application interface by selecting one of the widgets that supports the IoT data collection and visualization, e.g., Number, and connect it to the required sensor.

![ Adding the Number widget to a 2Smart Cloud platform](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%202400%201800'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![ Adding the Number widget to a 2Smart Cloud platform](https://webbylab.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/2-1.png)

Adding the Number widget to a 2Smart Cloud platform.

![ Viewing the interfaces of connected devices through the MyBox dashboard](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%202400%201800'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![ Viewing the interfaces of connected devices through the MyBox dashboard](https://webbylab.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/3-1.png)

Viewing the interfaces of connected devices through the MyBox dashboard.

All in all, most of [our IoT projects](https://webbylab.com/cases/?category=iot) enable the Internet of Things data collection and analysis. When building our products, our team aims to facilitate decision-making and real-time insights gathering through robust IoT-based data collection tools.

![share-linked](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)

![share-em](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![share-em](/wp-content/themes/webbylab/assets/images/share-em.svg)

IoT devices can collect various amounts of data depending on the particular device and its application area. For example, gadgets like smart home appliances may gather small amounts of data, while others, like industrial sensors, collect significantly more data.

There are several modes of  IoT-enabled data collection. They include real-time, event-based, periodic, and on-demand data gathering.

You can collect data from IoT devices by following the next steps:  
1. Connect the IoT devices to a network  
2. Select the IoT data collection mode  
3. Launch the IoT data collection system  
4. Filter and process the IoT data  
5. Visualize the IoT data using dashboards or apps

You can collect environmental data on temperature, humidity, light, air quality, and beyond, as well as equipment and automation data on the system’s performance, usage, wear and tear, status, and battery life. Besides that, you can get information from motion and sound sensors as well as GPS trackers and electricity, gas, or water meters.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Data collection involves using sensors to gather real-time information from physical environments, enabling constant monitoring and analysis in IoT applications.
Collected data is transmitted securely to cloud servers via IoT gateways, ensuring reliable communication and data integrity for further processing and analysis.
Data is processed and analyzed using advanced algorithms to extract meaningful insights, identify trends, and support decision-making in various applications.
Processed insights can be sent back to devices, enabling automated responses and adjustments based on real-time data analysis, enhancing operational efficiency.
